one_line_takeaway: REACT is a lightweight potential-field crash-avoidance/warning framework for autonomous driving that reports 0% false-alarm and 0% miss with sub-50 ms latency on a small on-vehicle test, but it is an operational road-safety (not cyber-security) contribution validated on only 8 trials per scenario.
key_finding: On the on-vehicle experiments (Table 4, 8 tests each), REACT achieved 0% false-alarm rate and 0% miss rate versus 25% / 25% for the human-driver baseline, with omnidirectional prediction coverage. REACT's warnings aligned closely with human anticipatory braking (time difference < 0.4 s in the car-following and intersection scenarios) while remaining accurate where human drivers erred (e.g., rear-approaching: REACT collision-warning time 2.7 s vs. a premature human 0.8 s). Per-scenario REACT warning times were CF=2.3 s, CI=1.7 s, RV=2.7 s, IC=4.4 s (vs. human CF=2.7, CI=0.6, RV=0.8, IC=4.8 s). Average response latency stayed ~35-50 ms on the embedded platform, and the paper reports 100% safe avoidance across the four scenarios. Qualitatively on highD, REACT and DRF detected lateral cut-in risk that longitudinal-only TTC/THW/RSS missed, and REACT further avoided DRF's delayed response and false re-triggering by capturing rear threats and post-cut-in relative-speed dynamics.
core_contribution: REACT (Runtime-Enabled Active Collision-avoidance Technique) is a closed-loop autonomous-driving safety framework that fuses risk assessment with active avoidance control. Its three stated contributions are: (1) a runtime multi-source risk-field model that abstracts each surrounding agent as a potential-field source whose intensity is driven by kinetic energy (mass, speed, participant-type coefficient), modulated by a direction term (cos of the angle between relative velocity and relative position) and a spatially decaying exponential over an anisotropic 'elliptical distance' that stretches the risk region along the motion direction; (2) an ego-centered spatial grid discretization that turns the continuous field into a 2D risk map, from which a normalized scalar global runtime risk value and eight directional sector risks are extracted to attribute a dominant risk direction; and (3) a lightweight three-level hierarchical warning/avoidance strategy (Level 0 Safe, Level 1 Warning/decelerate, Level 2 Emergency/brake+directional avoidance) with speed- and braking-adaptive thresholds, designed for real-time embedded deployment. Note: this is a functional/operational road-safety (crash-avoidance) paper, NOT a cyber-security paper; there is no adversary, attack, or defense against malicious manipulation anywhere in the text.
threat_model: No adversarial threat model. The paper addresses functional/operational safety, i.e., avoiding physical collisions with non-malicious traffic participants (leading/cut-in/rear/cross-traffic vehicles, cyclists, pedestrians) in dynamic mixed traffic. The 'risk' modeled is kinematic crash risk, not a security threat; there is no attacker, no data/sensor tampering, no spoofing, and no notion of an adversary attempting to defeat the system. The dataset cluster label 'Autonomous Vehicle Security' does not match the paper's actual scope.
limitations: (1) Very small on-vehicle evaluation: only 8 trials per group (16 total) across the four scenarios, on a single closed-course platform; no large-scale statistics or variance reported. (2) The highD comparison is qualitative (risk-level/timeline plots for individual cut-in and car-following episodes) rather than aggregate quantitative metrics over the 110k+ trajectories. (3) REACT outputs warnings/recommended actions to a human driver rather than direct control commands, so the '100% safe avoidance' reflects assisted human braking, not fully autonomous control; the authors themselves flag fully autonomous control and unstructured environments as future work. (4) Many model parameters are set heuristically (e.g., type coefficients truck=1.5/pedestrian=0.8, lateral scale b=5 m, longitudinal scale k=0.2 s, thresholds 0.3/0.7, lane stiffness 0.1-1), with no sensitivity analysis, despite the paper criticizing prior field methods for high parameter sensitivity. (5) Data availability is 'on request' only; no code or public benchmark release. (6) No adversarial/robustness evaluation (sensor noise, perception error, occlusion beyond the anecdotal blind-spot mention), so real-world reliability of the 0%/0% figures is unestablished.

datasets_used: highD real-world highway dataset (Germany; >110,000 trajectories across 60 highway scenarios) for comparative validation

Self-collected on-vehicle closed-course trials across four scenarios (car-following braking, cut-in, rear-approaching, unsignalized intersection conflict) using two Changan CS55 SUVs and one COMS electric vehicle
quantitative_results: REACT on-vehicle (8 tests): 0% false-alarm rate, 0% miss rate; omnidirectional prediction coverage
Human-driver baseline (8 tests): 25% false-alarm rate, 25% miss rate
REACT collision warning times: CF=2.3 s, CI=1.7 s, RV=2.7 s, IC=4.4 s
Human-driver collision warning times: CF=2.7 s, CI=0.6 s, RV=0.8 s, IC=4.8 s
Warning-vs-driver-braking time difference < 0.4 s in car-following and intersection scenarios (consistent with human cognition)
Average response latency ~35-50 ms on embedded platform (paper claims < 50 ms real-time)
Reported 100% safe avoidance with zero false alarms or missed detections across the four scenarios
Base risk thresholds T1=0.3 and T2=0.7 (dynamically adjusted by relative speed and braking state)
baselines_compared: Natural human-driver risk perception (on-vehicle warning-time / false-alarm / miss-rate comparison)
